data.body:
PASS-1 review (criterion T1 — prior-art / novel-architecture justification). One grounded finding + affirmations. This is a coaching note, not a roadblock.

A T1 bright-line arguably fires (a migration/rollout of a security/signing scheme), so prior-art consideration is in scope. The finding is T1(a): the plan proposes to BUILD machinery that ALREADY EXISTS in this repo and the parent epic's design — i.e. it risks re-inventing/repackaging shipped functionality rather than consuming it.

FINDING (T1(a) — prior art already in-repo; reframe build → verify/document the existing mechanism):

- [ ] Reconcile this plan with the ALREADY-SHIPPED disable-config + coexistence-rollout before treating either as net-new work — both halves of the Scope appear to exist in the codebase and in the parent epic 5fd2's own design.
  - criteria: T1
  - location: plan Scope items 1 & 2 + Acceptance Criteria lines 1 & 2 (vs `src/rebar/config.py:260`, `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:651-662`, `docs/plan-review-gate.md:180`, `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:13-14`, `CLAUDE.md:301`)
  - evidence:
    - Scope #1 / AC1 ("A project-level disable-the-gate config; gate off => claim behaves exactly as today; fail-open … covered by a test"): the config flag ALREADY EXISTS — `verify.require_plan_review_for_claim: bool = False` (`src/rebar/config.py:260`), and the claim path already short-circuits to today's behavior when off (`transition.py:661-662` `if not on: return None`) AND fails OPEN on an unreadable config (`transition.py:652-660`). It is already TESTED (`tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py:44,233,238-239`) and DOCUMENTED, where `docs/plan-review-gate.md:180` states verbatim: "Turning it off is the rollback — an ordinary preference, no kill-switch needed." The parent epic's own SC#4 already specifies this preference (default OFF). So AC1 appears already-satisfied.
    - Scope #2 / AC2 ("old+new coexistence during rollout … older clones preserve-and-ignore the new events"): this rollout pattern is ALREADY ESTABLISHED and the new events already adopt it — `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:13-14` documents that `REVIEW_RESULT` "follows the preserved-and-ignored-by-older-clones rollout (upgrade reconcile hosts first)", backed by `CLAUDE.md:301`, `docs/event-schema.md:77`, and `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_event_schema_forward_compat.py`. The parent epic's child `db7b` already shipped this (epic comment 2026-06: "REVIEW_RESULT + attestation events follow rebar's preserved-and-ignored-by-older-clones rollout (fsck WARN; reconcile hosts upgrade first)").
  - suggested_fix: Re-scope from "build a disable-config + a rollback path + coexistence" to "VERIFY and DOCUMENT the existing mechanism, and fill the genuinely-missing residual." The real residual the originating T12 finding pointed at (epic comment, final_gate_cycle3) is narrower than the current Scope: the DEPLOY-ORDERING / cross-clone behavior of the SIGNATURE attestation event specifically (not REVIEW_RESULT, which is already handled) and what happens to already-emitted attestation events on a rebar DOWNGRADE to a reducer that doesn't know the type. If that is the gap, state it explicitly and point AC1/AC2 at the existing `verify.require_plan_review_for_claim` + `sidecar.py` rollout note rather than re-authoring them; if the intent is a DIFFERENT (deploy-time, not config-preference) kill-switch, justify why the existing config preference is insufficient — the parent epic explicitly decided "an ordinary config preference, not a deploy-time kill-switch, and no separate rollback mechanism is needed" (5fd2 SC#4), so a net-new mechanism must argue against that settled decision.

AFFIRMATIONS (T1):
- T1(b) anti-repackaging / Rule-of-Three: PASS — the plan introduces NO novel architectural pattern; it (re)uses rebar's existing config-preference and preserved-and-ignored event-rollout patterns. No novelty needing justification-vs-established-approach.
- T1(c) unverified capability assertions: PASS — the plan makes no "library supports X" capability claim; its references (the signature/REVIEW_RESULT-sidecar scheme, the TAG_DELTA/event-rollout note) are to in-repo mechanisms, all of which resolve to real source (config.py, transition.py, sidecar.py, event-schema forward-compat).
